sp-sec10-u6-L1-Q1.1: Both Voyager 1 and Voyager 2 launched in 1977, about two weeks apart, to take advantage of a rare planetary alignment.

sp-sec10-u6-L1-Q2.0: Carrying enough fuel for multiple planetary stops would be impossibly heavy; gravity assists provided free velocity changes.
sp-sec10-u6-L1-Q2.2: Remote steering cannot provide the massive speed changes needed to reach each planet; gravity assists supplied the energy.
sp-sec10-u6-L1-Q2.3: Both Voyagers visited multiple planets: Voyager 2 flew past Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune.

sp-sec10-u6-L1-Q5.0: Solar panels cannot generate enough power at the outer planets where sunlight is extremely dim.
sp-sec10-u6-L1-Q5.1: Chemical batteries would have been depleted decades ago; the Voyagers have operated for over 45 years.
sp-sec10-u6-L1-Q5.3: Nuclear fusion reactors do not exist in portable form; the Voyagers use radioisotope thermoelectric generators (RTGs).

sp-sec10-u6-L1-Q6.1: Voyager 1 crossed the heliopause into interstellar space in August 2012, confirmed by plasma density measurements.

sp-sec10-u6-L2-Q1.1: Sojourner landed as part of Mars Pathfinder in 1997, becoming the first wheeled vehicle to drive on Mars.

sp-sec10-u6-L2-Q2.0: Sojourner operated for about 83 days, exceeding its 7-day design life but not matching Opportunity's 15-year record.
sp-sec10-u6-L2-Q2.2: Curiosity has operated well beyond its 2-year primary mission but was not the rover famous for exceeding 90 days.
sp-sec10-u6-L2-Q2.3: Perseverance is still active but was not the rover that set the record for vastly exceeding a 90-day planned mission.

sp-sec10-u6-L2-Q6.1: Ingenuity demonstrated powered, controlled flight on Mars in April 2021, a first for any extraterrestrial body.

sp-sec10-u6-L2-Q7.0: Curiosity launched in 2011 and did not carry a helicopter; Ingenuity rode with Perseverance in 2021.
sp-sec10-u6-L2-Q7.1: Spirit operated from 2004 to 2010 and predated the Ingenuity helicopter by over a decade.
sp-sec10-u6-L2-Q7.3: Opportunity operated from 2004 to 2018 and was not designed to carry a helicopter.

sp-sec10-u6-L3-Q1.1: Cassini orbited Saturn from 2004 to 2017, spending 13 years studying the planet, its rings, and moons.

sp-sec10-u6-L3-Q2.0: Enceladus is an icy moon with no volcanic lava; the eruptions consist of water ice particles and vapor.
sp-sec10-u6-L3-Q2.2: Methane clouds are found on Titan, not Enceladus; Enceladus has water ice geysers.
sp-sec10-u6-L3-Q2.3: Dust storms occur on Mars; Enceladus shoots jets of water ice from its subsurface ocean.

sp-sec10-u6-L3-Q5.0: While Cassini did sample Saturn's atmosphere during its final plunge, the primary reason was planetary protection.
sp-sec10-u6-L3-Q5.2: Cassini's destruction was deliberate and planned, not accidental due to fuel depletion.
sp-sec10-u6-L3-Q5.3: Dramatic footage was a side benefit; the real reason was preventing biological contamination of potentially habitable moons.

sp-sec10-u6-L3-Q6.1: New Horizons launched in January 2006 and flew past Pluto in July 2015 after a 9.5-year journey.

sp-sec10-u7-L1-Q1.1: NASA was established on July 29, 1958 in response to the Soviet Union's Sputnik launch.

sp-sec10-u7-L1-Q2.0: The Moon landing occurred in 1969, over a decade after NASA was founded.
sp-sec10-u7-L1-Q2.2: The Challenger disaster occurred in 1986, nearly 30 years after NASA was created.
sp-sec10-u7-L1-Q2.3: WWII ended in 1945; NASA was not created until 1958 in direct response to the Soviet Sputnik launch.

sp-sec10-u7-L1-Q5.1: NASA's Commercial Crew Program enabled SpaceX's Crew Dragon to begin carrying astronauts to the ISS in 2020.

sp-sec10-u7-L1-Q6.0: The correct word is Aeronautics, not Air; the full name is National Aeronautics and Space Administration.
sp-sec10-u7-L1-Q6.2: NASA is not limited to North America; it is a national agency of the United States government.
sp-sec10-u7-L1-Q6.3: The correct name uses Aeronautics and Space Administration, not Aerospace and Science Authority.

sp-sec10-u7-L2-Q1.0: ESA is a cooperative organization of 22 European member states, not a single country's agency.

sp-sec10-u7-L2-Q2.0: Galileo was a NASA mission to Jupiter, not an ESA comet mission.
sp-sec10-u7-L2-Q2.2: Cassini was a NASA-ESA joint mission to Saturn, not a comet lander.
sp-sec10-u7-L2-Q2.3: Voyager was a NASA mission to the outer planets; ESA's Rosetta was the comet mission.

sp-sec10-u7-L2-Q5.0: Five members is far too few; ESA has grown to 22 member countries across Europe.
sp-sec10-u7-L2-Q5.1: Twelve underestimates ESA's membership; it currently has 22 member states.
sp-sec10-u7-L2-Q5.3: Fifty overstates ESA's membership; it has 22 member countries, not the entire European continent.

sp-sec10-u7-L3-Q1.0: India's Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan) cost approximately $74 million, a fraction of $1 billion.

sp-sec10-u7-L3-Q2.0: Many missions had visited Mars before; ISRO's achievement was succeeding on its very first attempt at remarkably low cost.
sp-sec10-u7-L3-Q2.2: Mangalyaan was an orbiter mission, not a rover landing; it studied Mars from orbit.
sp-sec10-u7-L3-Q2.3: Mangalyaan was an uncrewed orbiter; no Mars mission has been crewed to date.

sp-sec10-u7-L3-Q4.0: 1998 is too early; China's first crewed mission, Shenzhou 5, launched in October 2003.
sp-sec10-u7-L3-Q4.2: 2010 is seven years too late; Yang Liwei became the first Chinese astronaut in 2003.
sp-sec10-u7-L3-Q4.3: 2019 is sixteen years too late; China joined the human spaceflight club in 2003.

sp-sec10-u7-L3-Q6.1: China's Chang'e 4 mission landed on the far side of the Moon in January 2019, a historic first.

sp-sec10-u8-L1-Q1.1: Falcon 9 first stages are routinely landed and reflown, with some boosters completing over 20 flights.

sp-sec10-u8-L1-Q2.0: 1998 is too early; SpaceX was founded by Elon Musk in 2002.
sp-sec10-u8-L1-Q2.2: 2008 is the year Falcon 1 reached orbit, not when SpaceX was founded.
sp-sec10-u8-L1-Q2.3: 2012 is when Dragon first visited the ISS; SpaceX was founded a decade earlier in 2002.

sp-sec10-u8-L1-Q4.0: Falcon 9 boosters do not burn up; they are designed to survive reentry and land for reuse.
sp-sec10-u8-L1-Q4.1: Unlike expendable rockets, Falcon 9 boosters do not fall into the ocean; they perform powered landings.
sp-sec10-u8-L1-Q4.3: Falcon 9 uses propulsive landing, not parachutes; it fires its engine to decelerate and land vertically.

sp-sec10-u8-L1-Q5.1: SpaceX Crew Dragon first carried astronauts to the ISS in May 2020, becoming the first commercial crewed spacecraft.

sp-sec10-u8-L2-Q1.1: Jeff Bezos founded Blue Origin in 2000, funding it largely through his Amazon fortune.

sp-sec10-u8-L2-Q2.0: New Shepard is suborbital and cannot reach orbit; orbital launches are planned for the larger New Glenn rocket.
sp-sec10-u8-L2-Q2.1: New Shepard does not carry ISS cargo; it provides brief suborbital flights above the Karman line.
sp-sec10-u8-L2-Q2.3: New Shepard is a suborbital vehicle; Moon missions would require Blue Origin's much larger New Glenn rocket.

sp-sec10-u8-L2-Q5.0: Commercial spaceflight supplements space agencies, not replaces them; agencies still lead deep space exploration.
sp-sec10-u8-L2-Q5.2: Safety depends on implementation, not ownership; both public and private operators must meet rigorous standards.
sp-sec10-u8-L2-Q5.3: Commercial spaceflight benefits everyone through lower costs, more launches, and faster technology development.

sp-sec10-u9-L1-Q1.0: Artemis 1 was an uncrewed test flight in 2022; the first crewed Artemis mission is planned for later.

sp-sec10-u9-L1-Q2.0: Returning to the exact Apollo 11 site would not provide new scientific value; the south pole has water ice.
sp-sec10-u9-L1-Q2.1: The equator was where Apollo landed; Artemis targets the south pole for its unique resources.
sp-sec10-u9-L1-Q2.2: The far side is being explored by China; Artemis focuses on the south pole for potential water ice.

sp-sec10-u9-L1-Q5.1: Gateway is planned as a small space station in lunar orbit to support Artemis Moon missions.

sp-sec10-u9-L1-Q6.0: The south pole has rough terrain with craters, not smooth landing areas.
sp-sec10-u9-L1-Q6.1: The south pole actually has areas of permanent shadow, not maximum sunlight.
sp-sec10-u9-L1-Q6.3: Distance from Earth is essentially the same for all points on the Moon's surface.

sp-sec10-u9-L2-Q1.1: China completed the Tiangong space station with the Mengtian module in October 2022.

sp-sec10-u9-L2-Q2.0: China was never invited to join the ISS; US law (the Wolf Amendment) prohibited NASA cooperation with China.
sp-sec10-u9-L2-Q2.1: Tiangong is smaller than the ISS; the motivation was exclusion from the ISS partnership, not size.
sp-sec10-u9-L2-Q2.3: China was well aware of the ISS; the exclusion was a deliberate US policy decision.

sp-sec10-u9-L2-Q5.0: Both the US and China are actively involved, making it a multi-party competition.
sp-sec10-u9-L2-Q5.1: The US and China compete rather than cooperate on most space activities.
sp-sec10-u9-L2-Q5.3: The competition extends well beyond satellites to include Moon landing, space stations, and Mars exploration.

sp-sec10-u9-L2-Q6.1: China has publicly announced plans to land astronauts on the Moon by approximately 2030.

sp-sec10-u9-L3-Q1.1: Starship is designed as a fully reusable vehicle capable of carrying crew and cargo to Mars.

sp-sec10-u9-L3-Q2.0: Two weeks is far too short; the minimum energy transfer to Mars takes 6-9 months.
sp-sec10-u9-L3-Q2.1: Three months is possible with advanced propulsion but a standard Hohmann transfer takes 6-9 months.
sp-sec10-u9-L3-Q2.3: Five years is far too long; even with inefficient trajectories, Mars trips take under a year.

sp-sec10-u9-L3-Q5.0: Mars is the ultimate goal; the Moon serves as a proving ground for Mars-bound technologies.
sp-sec10-u9-L3-Q5.2: Mars missions have not been cancelled; NASA's long-term plan includes both Moon and Mars exploration.
sp-sec10-u9-L3-Q5.3: The Moon is targeted for technology testing, not because of superior landing sites.

sp-sec10-u10-L1-Q4.0: Two people walked on the Moon during Apollo 11 alone; six more missions landed additional astronauts.
sp-sec10-u10-L1-Q4.1: Six is the number of successful Moon landings, not the number of individual moonwalkers.
sp-sec10-u10-L1-Q4.3: Twenty-four is the total number of astronauts who flew to the Moon, but only 12 walked on its surface.

sp-sec10-u10-L1-Q6.1: The Space Shuttle operated from 1981 to 2011, spanning 30 years and 135 missions.

sp-sec10-u10-L2-Q2.0: NASA has not landed a rover on the far side; China's CNSA achieved this first with Chang'e 4.
sp-sec10-u10-L2-Q2.1: ESA has not landed a rover on the Moon's far side; this was a Chinese achievement.
sp-sec10-u10-L2-Q2.3: ISRO has focused on lunar orbiters and south pole missions, not far-side landings.

sp-sec10-u10-L2-Q5.1: ISRO's Mars Orbiter Mission cost approximately $74 million, making it the cheapest Mars mission in history.

sp-sec10-u10-L2-Q6.0: Solar panels are useless at the Voyagers' distance where sunlight is essentially nonexistent.
sp-sec10-u10-L2-Q6.1: Hydrogen fuel cells would have been exhausted decades ago during the 45+ year mission.
sp-sec10-u10-L2-Q6.3: Rechargeable batteries cannot last decades in deep space; RTGs provide steady power from plutonium decay.
